file_name: Aloraini - 2023 - Two-stream convolutional networks for skin cancer classification.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a two-stream convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture with Xception backbones to classify skin cancer types using the HAM10000 dataset. The method combines RGB images and gradient images as inputs to detect and classify skin cancer, achieving 96.67% accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes a novel two-stream convolutional neural network approach for skin cancer classification, details the methodology including data augmentation and model architecture, and reports original experimental results and performance metrics on the HAM10000 dataset.

title: Two-stream convolutional networks for skin cancer classification
brief_summary: This study proposes a novel two-stream convolutional neural network architecture to classify seven types of skin cancer using the HAM10000 dataset. The method combines standard RGB images with gradient images, extracted via the Sobel operator, to capture high-frequency details and enhance classification accuracy. The two streams utilize Xception backbones and are fused using a sum fusion layer after extensive data augmentation to address class imbalance. The model was evaluated using precision, recall, F1-score, and accuracy metrics on a split of the dataset. The proposed approach achieved a state-of-the-art accuracy of 96.67%, outperforming several existing single-stream and transformer-based methods. The results demonstrate that integrating gradient information with RGB inputs significantly improves skin cancer detection performance.

all_task_types.target: Seven discrete skin cancer classes (Actinic keratoses, Basal cell carcinoma, Benign keratosis-like lesions, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Melanocytic nevi, Vascular lesions)
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of skin cancer types using the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (lesion classes) rather than continuous values. Performance was evaluated using accuracy (96.67%), precision, recall, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Human Against Machine)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset consisting of 10,015 skin lesion images belonging to seven classes. The dataset was used to train and evaluate the proposed two-stream convolutional neural network for skin cancer classification.

use.training: Used for training the model after extensive data augmentation to balance class distribution. The dataset was split into 80% for training.
use.validation: Used to evaluate the model while tuning hyperparameters. The dataset was split into 10% for validation.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the final tuned model. The dataset was split into 10% for testing.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To report average performance metrics and reduce variance in the evaluation of the proposed model.
To analyze classification performance per class, specifically identifying misclassifications between similar classes (e.g., Bcc vs. Df/Mel).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score over 10 experimental runs, with results reported as averages.

HAM10000.notes: Aladhadh et al. performed better in Precision and F1, but the proposed approach outperformed all in Accuracy. Chaturvedi et al. used Xception with one stream (RGB only) for comparison.

architecture_used.main_model: Two-stream convolutional neural network with Xception backbones
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained Xception models were evaluated (Xception, ResNet101, MobileNets), with Xception selected as the backbone. Specific pretraining weights (e.g., ImageNet) are not explicitly stated, but 'pre-trained' is mentioned.

architecture_details.structure: Two parallel streams processing RGB images and gradient images (extracted via Sobel operator). Each stream consists of Xception entry, middle, and exit flows (36 convolutional layers).
architecture_details.fusion: Sum fusion layer combining the two streams.

training_details.data_augmentation: Rotation (0-45 degrees), width/height shift (range 0.2), shear (range 0.2), zoom (range 0.2), horizontal and vertical flip. Classes Akiec, Bcc, Bkl, Df, Nv, Vasc augmented to 1500 images; Mel remained at 6705.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Two-stream convolutional neural network with Xception backbone (proposed)
preprocessing_techniques: Gradient image extraction using Sobel gradient operator to capture high-frequency information (horizontal, vertical, and depth gradients) from RGB images
Data augmentation to address class imbalance: Rotation (0-45 degrees), width/height shift (range 0.2), shear (range 0.2), zoom (range 0.2), and horizontal/vertical flip

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to classes Akiec, Bcc, Bkl, Df, Nv, and Vasc to augment them to 1500 images each; Mel class was not augmented (remained at 6705 images)

conclusions: The proposed two-stream convolutional neural network, which combines RGB images with gradient images extracted via the Sobel operator, achieves a state-of-the-art accuracy of 96.67% on the HAM10000 dataset.
Integrating gradient images, which contain high-frequency information, with RGB images enhances classification accuracy by revealing more details of skin cancer lesions.
The two-stream architecture improves classification accuracy compared to single-stream approaches, as demonstrated by the performance gain over the single-stream Xception model.
The Basal cell carcinoma (Bcc) class achieved the lowest classification performance due to its visual similarity to Dermatofibroma (Df) and Melanoma (Mel) classes, leading to misclassifications.
The Vascular lesion (Vasc) class achieved the highest classification performance among all classes.
All seven skin cancer classes achieved an F1-score greater than 90%, indicating excellent overall classification performance.
While the proposed approach achieved the highest accuracy, the Aladhadh et al. approach performed better in terms of precision and F1-score.
Extensive data augmentation was necessary to address the class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset, particularly for underrepresented classes like Akiec, Bcc, Bkl, Df, Nv, and Vasc.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have significant class imbalance (e.g., Melanoma has 6705 samples vs. Vascular lesions with 142) and potential selection bias from specific dermatological centers, limiting generalizability to other populations.

The paper acknowledges that the proposed model requires a large number of training parameters (46,443,735) and a very large training dataset, noting these requirements are 'very expensive and therefore not practical' for deployment.

The Basal cell carcinoma (Bcc) class achieved the lowest classification performance due to visual similarity with Dermatofibroma (Df) and Melanoma (Mel), leading to misclassifications between these classes.

While the proposed approach achieved the highest accuracy (96.67%), the Aladhadh et al. approach performed better in terms of precision (96.00% vs 95.83%) and F1-score (97.00% vs 96.38%), indicating a trade-off between accuracy and other metrics.

The model was evaluated using a single split (80% training, 10% validation, 10% testing) repeated 10 times; there is no external validation on independent datasets to confirm robustness across different clinical settings.

Data augmentation was heavily relied upon to balance classes, augmenting underrepresented classes to 1500 images each, which may introduce synthetic bias or overfitting to augmented patterns rather than true biological variance.
